mn4:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn4:10.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:10.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while overcome by sloth and torpor, owing to the fault of being overcome by sloth and torpor those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:10.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while overcome by sloth and torpor.
mn4:10.4: I am free of sloth and torpor.
mn4:10.5: Of those noble ones who are free of sloth and torpor and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:10.6: Seeing this freedom from sloth and torpor in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:11.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:11.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while agitated, with uncalmed minds, owing to the fault of agitation and an uncalmed mind those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:11.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while agitated, with an uncalmed mind.
mn4:11.5: Of those noble ones who have calmed minds [*vūpasantacittā*] and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:11.6: Seeing this state of a calmed mind [*vūpasantacittataṁ*] in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:12.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:12.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while doubtful and uncertain, owing to the fault of doubt and uncertainty those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:12.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while doubtful and uncertain.
mn4:12.5: Of those noble ones who have crossed beyond doubt and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:12.6: Seeing in myself this state of having crossed beyond doubt, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:13.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:13.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while praising themselves and disparaging others, owing to the fault of self-praise and disparagement of others those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:13.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while praising myself and disparaging others.
mn4:13.4: I neither praise myself nor disparage others.
mn4:13.5: Of those noble ones who neither praise themselves nor disparage others and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:13.6: Seeing in myself this state of neither self-praise nor disparagement of others, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:14.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:14.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while timid and fearful by nature, owing to the fault of timidity and fearful disposition those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:14.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while timid and fearful by nature.
mn4:14.5: Of those noble ones who are free from shuddering and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:14.6: Seeing this freedom from shuddering in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:15.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:15.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while desiring gain, honor, and fame, owing to the fault of desiring gain, honor, and fame those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:15.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while desiring gain, honor, and fame.
mn4:15.5: Of those noble ones who are of few desires and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:15.6: Seeing this state of fewness of desires in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:16.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:16.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while lazy and lacking energy, owing to the fault of laziness and deficient energy those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:16.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while lazy and lacking energy.
mn4:16.5: Of those noble ones whose energy is aroused and who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:16.6: Seeing this state of aroused energy in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:17.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:17.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while forgetful and without full awareness [*asampajānā*], owing to the fault of forgetfulness and lack of full awareness [*asampajānā*] those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:17.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while forgetful and without full awareness [*asampajāno*].
mn4:17.5: Of those noble ones who have *sati* established [*upaṭṭhitassatī*] and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:17.6: Seeing in myself this state of *sati* established [*upaṭṭhitassatitaṁ*], brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:18.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:18.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while without *samādhi* [*asamāhitā*], with scattered minds [*vibbhantacittā*], owing to the fault of lacking *samādhi* [*asamāhita*] and having a scattered mind [*vibbhantacitta*] those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:18.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while without *samādhi* [*asamāhito*], with a scattered mind [*vibbhantacitto*].
mn4:18.5: Of those noble ones who are endowed with *samādhi* [*samādhisampannā*] and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:18.6: Seeing in myself accomplishment in *samādhi* [*samādhisampadaṁ*], brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:19.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:19.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while poor in discernment [*paññā*] and dull-witted, owing to the fault of poor discernment [*paññā*] and dullness those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:19.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while poor in discernment [*paññā*] and dull-witted.
mn4:19.5: Of those noble ones who are endowed with discernment [*paññāsampannā*] and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:19.6: Seeing in myself accomplishment in discernment [*paññāsampadaṁ*], brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:2.1: Then Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin went to the *Bhagavā*. Having gone up to him, he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*.
mn4:2.10: “So it is, brahmin, so it is, brahmin.
mn4:2.11: Indeed, brahmin, remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths are hard to endure; seclusion [*pavivekaṁ*] is difficult; it is hard to delight in being alone.
mn4:2.12: I suppose the forests carry off the mind of a *bhikkhu* who has not gained *samādhi*.
mn4:2.2: When they had concluded their courteous and memorable conversation, he sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, Jāṇussoṇi the brahmin said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn4:2.3: “Those sons of good families, Master Gotama, who have gone forth from home to homelessness out of faith in Master Gotama — Master Gotama is their leader, Master Gotama is of great help to them, Master Gotama is the one who encourages them;
mn4:2.4: and that following of Master Gotama follows his view.”
mn4:2.5: “So it is, brahmin, so it is, brahmin.
mn4:2.6: Those sons of good families, brahmin, who have gone forth from home to homelessness out of faith in me — I am their leader, I am of great help to them, I am the one who encourages them;
mn4:2.7: and that following of mine follows my view.”
mn4:2.8: “Indeed, Master Gotama, remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths are hard to endure; seclusion [*pavivekaṁ*] is difficult; it is hard to delight in being alone.
mn4:2.9: I suppose the forests carry off the mind of a *bhikkhu* who has not gained *samādhi*.”
mn4:20.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:20.10: ‘Surely this is that fear and terror coming.’
mn4:20.11: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:20.12: ‘Why should I dwell merely expecting fear?
mn4:20.13: What if, in whatever posture that fear and terror comes to me, I were to dispel that fear and terror while remaining in that very posture?’
mn4:20.14: Then, brahmin, that fear and terror comes to me while I am walking.
mn4:20.15: So, brahmin, I do not stand, sit down, or lie down until I have dispelled that fear and terror while still walking.
mn4:20.16: Then, brahmin, that fear and terror comes to me while I am standing.
mn4:20.17: So, brahmin, I do not walk, sit down, or lie down. I dispel that fear and terror while still standing.
mn4:20.18: Then, brahmin, that fear and terror comes to me while I am sitting.
mn4:20.19: So, brahmin, I do not lie down, stand up, or walk until I have dispelled that fear and terror while still sitting.
mn4:20.2: ‘What if, on those nights that are recognized and marked out—
mn4:20.20: Then, brahmin, that fear and terror comes to me while I am lying down.
mn4:20.21: So, brahmin, I do not sit up, stand up, or walk until I have dispelled that fear and terror while still lying down.
mn4:20.3: the fourteenth, the fifteenth, and the eighth of the fortnight—
mn4:20.4: I were to dwell in such lodgings as those frightening and hair-raising shrine-places in parks, shrine-places in forests, and shrine-places at trees? Perhaps I might see that fear and terror.’
mn4:20.5: Then, brahmin, at a later time, on those nights that are recognized and marked out—
mn4:20.6: the fourteenth, the fifteenth, and the eighth of the fortnight—
mn4:20.7: I dwell in such lodgings as those frightening and hair-raising shrine-places in parks, shrine-places in forests, and shrine-places at trees.
mn4:20.8: And there, while I am dwelling, a deer comes, or a peacock knocks down a twig, or the wind stirs up a rustling of fallen leaves.
mn4:20.9: Then this thought occurred to me, brahmin:
mn4:21.1: But there are, brahmin, some ascetics and brahmins who, while it is night, perceive it as day, and while it is day, perceive it as night.
mn4:21.2: I call this a dwelling in delusion on the part of those ascetics and brahmins.
mn4:21.3: But I, brahmin, while it is night, perceive it as night; while it is day, I perceive it as day.
mn4:21.4: If anyone, brahmin, speaking rightly, should say:
mn4:21.5: ‘A being not subject to delusion has arisen in the world for the welfare of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of compassion for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of devas and humans,’
mn4:21.6: speaking rightly, he would say this of me: ‘A being not subject to delusion has arisen in the world for the welfare of the many, for the happiness of the many, out of compassion for the world, for the benefit, welfare, and happiness of devas and humans.’
mn4:22-26.1: And my energy, brahmin, was aroused and unsluggish, my *sati* was established and undeluded, my body was tranquil and unagitated, my mind [*citta*] was endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*], gathered [*ekaggaṁ*].
mn4:22-26.2: Quite secluded from sensual pleasures, secluded from unwholesome *dhammas*, I entered and dwelt in the first *jhāna*, which has thought [*savitakkaṁ*] and examination [*savicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of seclusion [*vivekajaṁ*].
mn4:22-26.3: With the stilling of thought [*vitakka*] and examination [*vicāra*], I entered and dwelt in the second *jhāna*, which has inner serenity, unification [*ekodibhāvaṁ*] of mind, without thought [*avitakkaṁ*], without examination [*avicāraṁ*], with *pīti* and *sukha* born of *samādhi*.
mn4:22-26.4: And with the fading of *pīti*, I dwelt in equanimity [*upekkhā*], with *sati* [*sato*] and full awareness [*sampajāno*], and I experienced *sukha* with the body. I entered and dwelt in the third *jhāna*, of which the noble ones declare: ‘One who dwells in *sukha*, equanimous [*upekkhā*], with *sati* [*satimā*].’
mn4:22-26.5: With the abandoning of *sukha* and the abandoning of *dukkha*, and with the earlier disappearance of joy and distress, I entered and dwelt in the fourth *jhāna*, which is neither painful nor pleasant, and is the purity of equanimity [*upekkhā*] and *sati* [*upekkhāsatipārisuddhiṁ*].
mn4:27.1: When my mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, I directed the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of recollecting former lives. I recollected many kinds of former lives,
mn4:27.2: that is: one birth, two births, three births, four births, five births, ten births, twenty births, thirty births, forty births, fifty births, a hundred births, a thousand births, a hundred thousand births, many eons of world-contraction, many eons of world-expansion, many eons of world-contraction and expansion: ‘There I was so named, of such a clan, of such an appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a lifespan. Passing away from there, I arose elsewhere. There too I was so named, of such a clan, of such an appearance, with such food, experiencing such *sukha* and *dukkha*, with such a lifespan. Passing away from there, I reappeared here.’ Thus, with aspects and details, I recollected many kinds of former lives.
mn4:28.1: This, brahmin, was the first knowledge attained by me in the first watch of the night.
mn4:28.2: Blindness [*avijjā*] was destroyed, knowledge arose, darkness was destroyed, light arose — as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn4:29.1: When my mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, I directed the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the passing away and reappearance of beings.
mn4:29.2: With the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, I saw beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, fair and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination. I understood beings as going according to their *kamma*: ‘These worthy beings, who were endowed with bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, and mental misconduct, who reviled the noble ones, held wrong view, and undertook action based on wrong view — with the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a state of loss, a bad destination, downfall, hell. But these worthy beings, who were endowed with bodily good conduct, verbal good conduct, and mental good conduct, who did not revile the noble ones, held right view, and undertook action based on right view — with the breakup of the body, after death, they have arisen in a good destination, a heavenly world.’ Thus, with the divine eye, purified and surpassing the human, I saw beings passing away and reappearing, inferior and superior, fair and ugly, in a good destination and a bad destination. I understood beings as going according to their *kamma*.
mn4:3.1: Before my awakening too, brahmin, while I was still a bodhisatta, not yet fully awakened, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:3.2: ‘Indeed, remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths are hard to endure; seclusion [*pavivekaṁ*] is difficult; it is hard to delight in being alone.
mn4:3.3: I suppose the forests carry off the mind of a *bhikkhu* who has not gained *samādhi*.’
mn4:30.1: This, brahmin, was the second knowledge attained by me in the middle watch of the night.
mn4:30.2: Blindness [*avijjā*] was destroyed, knowledge arose, darkness was destroyed, light arose — as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn4:31.1: When my mind [*citta*] was thus endowed with *samādhi*, purified, bright, unblemished, rid of defilements, pliant, workable, steady, and attained to imperturbability, I directed the mind [*citta*] to the knowledge of the destruction of the *āsavas*.
mn4:31.2: I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the path leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn4:31.3: I directly knew as it truly is: ‘These are the *āsavas*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of the *āsavas*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of the *āsavas*’; I directly knew as it truly is: ‘This is the path leading to the cessation of the *āsavas*.’
mn4:32.1: For me, knowing thus and seeing thus, the mind [*citta*] was liberated from the *āsava* of sensuality [*kāmāsavā*], the mind [*citta*] was liberated from the *āsava* of *bhava* [*bhavāsavā*], the mind [*citta*] was liberated from the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavā*].
mn4:32.2: When it was liberated, there was the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
mn4:32.3: I directly knew: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn4:33.1: This, brahmin, was the third knowledge attained by me in the last watch of the night.
mn4:33.2: Blindness [*avijjā*] was destroyed, knowledge arose, darkness was destroyed, light arose — as happens for one dwelling diligent, ardent, and resolute.
mn4:34.2: ‘Perhaps even now the ascetic Gotama is not free from passion, not free from hatred, not free from delusion; that is why he resorts to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths.’
mn4:34.3: But this should not be seen in that way, brahmin.
mn4:34.4: Seeing two benefits, brahmin, I resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths—
mn4:34.5: seeing a pleasant dwelling here and now for myself, and having compassion for future generations.”
mn4:35.1: “Future generations have indeed been shown compassion by Master Gotama, as is fitting for an *arahant*, a perfectly awakened one.
mn4:35.2: Excellent, Master Gotama. Excellent, Master Gotama.
mn4:35.3: Just as if someone were to set upright what had been overturned, or reveal what was concealed, or point out the way to one who was lost, or hold up an oil lamp in the darkness so that those with eyes could see forms, so too the *dhamma* has been made clear by Master Gotama in many ways.
mn4:35.4: I go for refuge to Master Gotama, to the *dhamma*, and to the *saṅgha* of *bhikkhus* [*bhikkhusaṅghañca*].
mn4:35.5: May Master Gotama remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge from this day forth, for life.”
mn4:35.6: The fourth is finished: Fear and Terror.
mn4:4.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:4.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while their bodily conduct is impure, owing to the fault of impure bodily conduct those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:4.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths with impure bodily conduct.
mn4:4.5: Of those noble ones who have pure bodily conduct and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:4.6: Seeing this purity of bodily conduct in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:5.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:5.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while their verbal conduct is impure …
mn4:5.3: while their mental conduct is impure …
mn4:5.4: while their livelihood is impure, owing to the fault of impure livelihood those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:6.1: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths with impure livelihood.
mn4:7.1: Of those noble ones who have pure livelihood and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:7.2: Seeing this purity of livelihood in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:8.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:8.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while covetous, with intense passion for sensual pleasures, owing to the fault of covetousness and intense passion for sensual pleasures those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:8.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while covetous, with intense passion for sensual pleasures.
mn4:8.5: Of those noble ones who are without covetousness and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:8.6: Seeing this freedom from covetousness in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
mn4:9.1: Then, brahmin, this thought occurred to me:
mn4:9.2: ‘Whatever ascetics or brahmins there may be who resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while their mind is affected by ill will and their intentions are corrupted, owing to the fault of ill will and corrupted intentions those ascetics and brahmins indeed summon up unwholesome fear and terror.
mn4:9.3: But I do not resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths while my mind is affected by ill will and my intentions are corrupted.
mn4:9.4: I have a mind of goodwill [*mettacitto*].
mn4:9.5: Of those noble ones who have a mind of goodwill [*mettacittā*] and resort to remote lodgings in the wilderness and forest depths, I am one.’
mn4:9.6: Seeing this state of a mind of goodwill [*mettacittataṁ*] in myself, brahmin, I grew still more confident in dwelling in the wilderness.
